definition: The simulation of human intelligence by machines — enabling systems to learn, reason, and make decisions.

A hypothetical AI system capable of understanding, learning, and applying knowledge across any domain — matching or exceeding human cognitive abilities. Unlike today's narrow AI, AGI would reason broadly and adapt to entirely new situations. AGI does not yet exist.
A subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data and improve over time without being explicitly programmed.
A type of machine learning using neural networks with many layers to model complex patterns in data (images, text, audio).
AI models trained on massive text datasets that can generate, summarize, translate, and reason about human language. Examples: GPT-4, Claude, Gemini.
A computing system loosely inspired by the human brain, composed of layers of interconnected nodes that process information.
The branch of AI that enables machines to understand, interpret, and generate human language.
AI systems that create new content — text, images, code, audio, video — rather than just analyzing existing data.
The input or instruction you give to an AI model to get a desired response. The quality of the prompt directly affects output quality.
The practice of designing and refining prompts to get optimal results from AI models — a critical skill for effective AI use.
Providing a few examples in the prompt so the AI understands the pattern and format you expect.
Asking the AI to perform a task without providing any examples — relying on the model's pre-trained knowledge.
A prompting technique that encourages the model to show its reasoning step-by-step, improving accuracy on complex tasks.
A technique that combines LLMs with external knowledge retrieval — the model searches a database before generating a response, reducing hallucinations.
Adapting a pre-trained AI model to a specific task or domain by training it further on a specialized dataset.
Using knowledge learned from one task to improve performance on a different but related task — the foundation of modern AI.
When an AI model generates information that sounds plausible but is factually incorrect or fabricated.
The basic units of text that LLMs process. A token can be a word, part of a word, or punctuation. Token limits define how much text a model can handle.
The maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) that a model can process in a single conversation. Larger windows = more context awareness.
A parameter that controls randomness in AI output. Low temperature = more predictable/focused. High temperature = more creative/varied.
A numerical representation of text, images, or data in a high-dimensional space — enables AI to understand similarity and relationships.
A database optimized for storing and searching embeddings — essential for RAG, semantic search, and recommendation systems.
The neural network architecture behind modern LLMs. Uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequences of data efficiently.
A technique that lets the model focus on the most relevant parts of the input when generating each piece of output.
An AI system that can autonomously plan, reason, use tools, and take actions to accomplish goals — going beyond simple Q&A.
AI systems designed to act independently with minimal human oversight — planning multi-step tasks, calling APIs, and making decisions.
The ability of an AI model to invoke external tools, APIs, or functions to gather data or perform actions beyond text generation.
Architectures where multiple AI agents collaborate, each with specialized roles, to solve complex tasks together.
Safety mechanisms and rules that constrain AI behavior — preventing harmful, biased, or off-topic outputs.
Policies, processes, and controls for responsible AI adoption — covering ethics, compliance, risk, and accountability.
Systematic errors in AI output caused by imbalanced or skewed training data — can lead to unfair or discriminatory results.
The ability to understand and articulate why an AI model made a particular decision — critical for trust and compliance.
Gradual degradation of model performance over time as real-world data patterns shift away from the training data.
The practice of deploying, monitoring, and managing machine learning models in production — DevOps for AI.
The process of using a trained model to make predictions or generate outputs on new data — what happens when you 'use' an AI.
The time it takes for an AI model to generate a response after receiving input. Lower latency = faster responses.
An interface that lets applications interact with AI models programmatically — send prompts, receive responses.
An AI assistant embedded in workflows (coding, writing, analysis) that suggests, completes, or automates tasks in real-time.
A type of generative AI that creates images by gradually removing noise from random data — used by DALL·E, Stable Diffusion, Midjourney.
AI systems that can process and generate multiple types of data — text, images, audio, video — in a single model.
AI models and tools released with open licenses, allowing anyone to use, modify, and build upon them. Examples: Llama, Mistral, Hugging Face.
Artificially generated data used to train AI models when real data is scarce, sensitive, or expensive to collect.
A training technique where human preferences are used to fine-tune AI models — key to making LLMs helpful and safe.
Running AI models locally on devices (phones, sensors, IoT) rather than in the cloud — enabling faster, private, offline inference.
An organizational mindset where AI is a core capability woven into every process, not just an add-on — the future of software delivery.
Developing and deploying AI systems that are fair, transparent, accountable, and aligned with human values.
A security vulnerability where malicious inputs trick an AI into ignoring its instructions or leaking sensitive information.
A large AI model pre-trained on broad data that serves as a base for many downstream tasks. GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, and Llama are all foundation models.
The technique of connecting an AI model's output to verified, factual sources — reducing hallucinations by anchoring responses in real data.
The process of breaking text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters) that a language model can process. Different models use different tokenizers.
Hidden instructions given to an AI model that define its personality, rules, and behavior — set by the developer, not visible to the end user.
Breaking a complex task into a series of sequential prompts, where the output of one becomes the input of the next — enabling multi-step reasoning.
The process of coordinating multiple AI components — models, tools, memory, and APIs — into a cohesive workflow or pipeline.
A structured representation of real-world entities and their relationships, used to give AI systems organized, queryable knowledge.
A standardized test or dataset used to evaluate and compare AI model performance — e.g., MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K.
A learnable weight in a neural network. Model size is often expressed in parameters — GPT-4 has trillions, while smaller models have billions.
Specialized processors optimized for AI workloads. GPUs (NVIDIA) and TPUs (Google) accelerate model training and inference by orders of magnitude over CPUs.
A development approach where you describe what you want in natural language and an AI assistant writes the code — making software creation accessible to non-developers.
An open standard that lets AI assistants connect to external tools and data sources through a universal interface — like USB-C for AI.
The field of AI that enables machines to interpret and understand visual information from images and video — used in autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, and more.
The process of tagging training data with correct labels so supervised AI models can learn from examples — a critical step in model development.
An automated workflow that collects, processes, transforms, and feeds data into AI models for training or inference.
The research field focused on ensuring AI systems reliably act in accordance with human intentions and values — especially critical as models become more capable.
AI systems that can operate independently for extended periods, making decisions and taking actions without human approval at each step.
A credential used to authenticate with platforms like GitHub from command lines, scripts, or apps — replacing passwords with scoped, expirable, and revocable tokens for secure programmatic access.
A unique secret string used to authenticate and authorize access to an AI service or API — must be kept secure and never hardcoded in source code.
A technique to reduce AI model size and speed up inference by lowering the precision of model weights (e.g., from 32-bit to 4-bit) — enabling large models to run on consumer hardware.
An efficient fine-tuning technique that trains only a small number of additional parameters instead of the full model — dramatically reducing compute and memory requirements.
The process of training a smaller, faster 'student' model to replicate the behavior of a larger 'teacher' model — compressing knowledge while maintaining quality.
A model architecture that routes each input to a subset of specialized sub-networks (experts), enabling massive models to run efficiently by activating only relevant parts.
Search based on the meaning and intent of a query rather than exact keyword matches — powered by embeddings and vector databases for more relevant results.
The process of splitting large documents into smaller, overlapping segments for RAG pipelines — chunk size and strategy significantly affect retrieval quality.
A design pattern where humans review, validate, or approve AI decisions at critical points — balancing automation with accountability and safety.
Compact language models (1B-7B parameters) optimized to run on edge devices and laptops — trading some capability for speed, privacy, and lower cost. Examples: Phi, Gemma, Llama 3.2.
Forcing an AI model to return responses in a specific format (JSON, XML, tables) rather than free text — critical for integrating AI into automated workflows.
Receiving AI model responses token-by-token in real-time as they are generated, rather than waiting for the complete response — improving perceived latency and user experience.
Controlling the number of API requests a user or application can make within a time period — preventing abuse and managing costs in AI services.
The systematic process of measuring AI system quality using metrics, test datasets, and human judgment — essential for ensuring models meet accuracy, safety, and performance standards.
A training approach where AI models are guided by a set of written principles (a 'constitution') to self-improve their responses — reducing the need for human feedback at scale.
Adversarial testing of AI systems to discover vulnerabilities, biases, and failure modes before deployment — a critical practice for AI safety and security.
A security attack where malicious data is injected into training datasets to manipulate AI model behavior — causing targeted errors or backdoors in the model.
Techniques used to bypass an AI model's safety restrictions and guardrails — a key threat that AI developers actively defend against with alignment and filtering.
The ability of an AI system to retain and reference information from earlier in a conversation or across sessions — enabling coherent, contextual interactions.
The iterative cycle an AI agent follows: observe the environment, reason about the next step, take an action, and evaluate the result — repeating until the goal is achieved.
Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment applied to AI — automating model testing, validation, and deployment pipelines to ship AI updates reliably and frequently.
An HTTP callback that automatically notifies your application when an event occurs — used in AI workflows to trigger actions when models finish processing or new data arrives.
A configuration value stored outside source code (e.g., API keys, model endpoints) — the standard way to manage secrets and settings across development, staging, and production environments.
Caching AI responses based on the meaning of queries rather than exact text matches — so similar questions return cached answers, reducing latency and API costs.
The maximum number of tokens (input + output) an AI model or API can handle in a single request — exceeding it causes truncation or errors. Managing token budgets is a key optimization skill.
A reusable prompt structure with placeholder variables that gets filled in dynamically at runtime — enabling consistent, scalable AI interactions across applications.
An organization's preparedness to adopt and benefit from AI — assessed across dimensions like data maturity, talent, infrastructure, governance, and culture.
The use of unauthorized or unapproved AI tools by employees without IT or security oversight — posing risks to data privacy, compliance, and organizational governance.
A component in RAG systems that searches and fetches the most relevant documents or chunks from a knowledge base to provide context for the AI model's response.
Decoding strategies that control how AI models select the next token. Top-K picks from the K most likely tokens; Top-P (nucleus sampling) picks from tokens whose cumulative probability reaches P.
Running two or more versions of an AI system simultaneously to compare performance — used to evaluate prompt changes, model upgrades, or feature variations with real users.
AI models specifically trained to think step-by-step before answering — spending more compute at inference time to solve complex math, logic, and coding problems. Examples: OpenAI o1/o3, DeepSeek R1.
A capability where AI models allocate extra processing time to reason through complex problems before responding — showing their internal chain-of-thought for transparency and accuracy.
The strategy of using more computational resources during inference (response generation) rather than just during training — enabling models to 'think harder' on difficult questions.
AI agents capable of interacting with computer interfaces — clicking buttons, typing text, navigating websites, and using desktop applications autonomously like a human user.

Multi-step AI research agents that autonomously search the web, read documents, synthesize findings, and produce comprehensive reports — going far beyond single-query search. Examples: OpenAI Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research.
Autonomous AI systems that can plan, write, test, debug, and deploy code end-to-end with minimal human intervention — the next evolution beyond code completion. Examples: Devin, Copilot Agent Mode.
An advanced RAG technique that combines knowledge graphs with vector retrieval — enabling AI to reason over structured relationships between entities, not just similar text chunks.
Storing and reusing the processed representation of frequently used system prompts or context — reducing latency and cost for repeated prompts by up to 90%.
A unified proxy layer that routes AI requests across multiple model providers — enabling load balancing, fallbacks, cost optimization, rate limiting, and observability for AI APIs.
An application built on top of existing AI APIs (OpenAI, Claude, etc.) that adds a specific UX, workflow, or domain focus — the most common pattern for AI startups.
AI systems that understand and generate natural speech in real-time — enabling conversational voice interfaces for customer service, assistants, and accessibility.
An AI-powered virtual replica of a real-world system, process, or environment — used for simulation, prediction, and optimization in manufacturing, healthcare, and urban planning.

A privacy-preserving ML technique where models are trained across multiple decentralized devices or servers without sharing raw data — keeping sensitive information local while building shared intelligence.
The practice of carefully designing what information goes into an AI model's context — selecting, ordering, and compressing the right data to maximize response quality within token limits.
An AI-driven process where autonomous agents plan and execute multi-step tasks — combining reasoning, tool use, memory, and human checkpoints to complete complex goals.
A documentation standard that describes an AI model's intended use, training data, performance metrics, limitations, and ethical considerations — promoting transparency and responsible deployment.
User-defined persistent preferences that customize how AI assistants behave across all conversations — specifying coding style, preferred languages, response format, and domain context.
Monitoring and analyzing AI system behavior in production — tracking latency, token usage, cost, output quality, hallucination rates, and user satisfaction to ensure reliable operation.
A technique that combines outputs from multiple different AI models to produce higher-quality responses — using diversity of model architectures as a strength for improved accuracy and robustness.
A performance metric measuring how fast an AI model generates output — higher TPS means faster responses, critical for real-time applications and user experience.
A file format for storing quantized AI models — optimized for fast local inference with tools like Ollama and LM Studio, enabling large models to run on consumer hardware.
Using AI models to evaluate the output of other AI models — automating quality assessment at scale when human evaluation is too slow or expensive.
A training technique that fine-tunes models on question-answer pairs alongside retrieved context — teaching the model to better use retrieved documents and ignore distractors.
